summary: Ronacher argues that Git’s UI is famously inconsistent, yet he doesn’t find it frustrating in practice. The reason is that Git teaches its underlying model early and transparently, so users learn the concept rather than just commands. That conceptual clarity and stability make the UI feel secondary, while good defaults and a strong core design keep workflows reliable over time. He contrasts this with systems where UI hides or distorts how things really work, which creates cognitive dissonance and errors. The conclusion is that Git earns forgiveness for its UI because its design is resilient, recoverable, and aligned with how it actually works.
key_points: Git’s UI is inconsistent, but daily use doesn’t feel confusing because the model is learned first.
Early Git exposed its internals, reinforcing a conceptual understanding that remains stable over time.
Good defaults and a single “blessed” workflow reduce friction despite UI rough edges.
Git’s core design enables recovery from mistakes and rarely leaves users stranded.
He wants more software where implementation and UI are aligned, unlike some modern apps.
money_quotes: Git didn't start as a version control system, it started as a concept that other tools could use to build a version control system on top.
The core concept of git has not changed.
Where the UI lacks consistency and maybe simplicity, the core design of git has both.
There has never been a situation where I failed so badly that I could not recover from it.
Neither did it break because I misused it nor did it broke out of its own volition.
Software where the way it works is a crucial part of the user experience.
And this is why I don't hate git.
key_insight: Git’s inconsistent UI is tolerable because its stable, transparent core model makes it reliable and recoverable when it matters.
